B.text:
## Recommendation

**Default to REST. Reach for GraphQL only if you have a specific, concrete driver for it** — typically many heterogeneous screens pulling overlapping data, multiple client types sharing one backend, or a UI that changes faster than your API team can ship endpoints.

For most mobile apps, REST (JSON over HTTP, resource-oriented, with a few tailored/aggregate endpoints) is the lower-risk, lower-cost choice. GraphQL is powerful but front-loads complexity that only pays off at a certain scale of UI variety.

## Why REST wins for the typical case

- **HTTP caching is free and mature.** GET responses cache at the CDN, proxy, and client (ETags, `Cache-Control`). On flaky mobile networks this is a big deal. GraphQL typically POSTs to a single endpoint, so you lose HTTP-layer caching and have to rebuild it (persisted queries, Apollo/Relay normalized cache) yourself.
- **Operational simplicity.** No schema layer, no resolver N+1 problems, no need to defend against expensive nested queries (depth/complexity limiting, cost analysis). Fewer ways to shoot yourself in the foot in production.
- **Easier to secure and reason about.** Per-endpoint authz and rate limiting is straightforward. In GraphQL, a single query can fan out across many resources, so authorization and abuse-prevention get genuinely harder.
- **Ubiquitous tooling and hiring.** Almost everyone knows it; debugging is `curl` + browser devtools.

## Why you'd choose GraphQL anyway

- **Round-trip reduction.** A complex screen (profile + posts + follower counts + settings) is one query instead of 4–5 REST calls. On high-latency mobile connections, fewer round-trips = faster, less battery/data. This is GraphQL's single best argument for mobile.
- **No over-/under-fetching.** Each screen requests exactly the fields it needs — meaningful when payload size matters.
- **Versionless evolution fits mobile's hard constraint.** You *cannot* force users to update; old app versions live in the wild for years. GraphQL's additive schema evolution (add fields, deprecate old ones) avoids the `/v1` → `/v2` endpoint sprawl. *(You can get most of this with disciplined additive REST too — but GraphQL makes it the default.)*
- **One backend, many clients.** If iOS, Android, and web all have different data shapes, GraphQL lets each ask for what it needs without you maintaining per-client endpoints.

## The pragmatic middle ground

A **BFF (Backend-for-Frontend)** — a REST layer that exposes a handful of screen-shaped, aggregated endpoints (`GET /home-feed` returning everything that screen needs) — captures most of GraphQL's round-trip benefit without the schema/resolver machinery. This is what a lot of teams actually land on.

## Decision shortcut

| Signal | Lean |
|---|---|
| Simple, resource-shaped data; small team; want CDN caching | **REST** |
| Many varied screens with overlapping data; multiple clients; UI iterates fast | **GraphQL** |
| Want GraphQL's benefits without the ops cost | **REST + BFF** |

If you can tell me the app's domain, team size/experience, and how varied the screens are, I'll give you a firmer call rather than a default.

F.text:
For most mobile apps, start with REST. Reach for GraphQL only when you have a specific, concrete reason — and "we might need flexibility later" is not one.

Here's the reasoning, weighted toward what actually matters on mobile:

**Why REST is the better default**

- **Caching is simpler and it matters more on mobile.** REST rides on HTTP semantics — CDN caching, `ETag`/`If-None-Match`, `Cache-Control` all work out of the box. That directly saves battery and bandwidth on flaky cell networks. GraphQL typically POSTs to a single endpoint, so you lose HTTP-layer and CDN caching and have to rebuild it in the client (normalized cache) or at the edge with persisted queries.
- **Lower operational complexity.** No schema layer, no resolver wiring, no N+1 query traps, no extra gateway to run and monitor. Faster to ship and easier to debug with plain `curl` and standard observability tools.
- **The ecosystem assumes it.** Auth, rate limiting, logging, API gateways, and monitoring are all REST-shaped by default.

**When GraphQL genuinely wins for mobile**

- **Many screens with very different data shapes, over slow networks.** GraphQL lets the client fetch exactly the fields it needs in one round trip, avoiding both over-fetching (wasted bytes) and under-fetching (waterfall requests). On 3G/spotty LTE this is a real, measurable UX win.
- **Multiple clients evolving at different speeds.** iOS, Android, and web each wanting different slices of the same data is GraphQL's sweet spot.
- **You can't force app updates.** Old app versions linger on phones for months or years. GraphQL's additive schema evolution (deprecate fields, never version the URL) handles long-lived clients more gracefully than REST's `/v1` → `/v2` churn — though disciplined REST versioning also works.

**My recommendation**

Default to REST. Choose GraphQL only if at least two of these are already true: you have 3+ distinct client types, screens with highly variable data needs that cause over/under-fetching today, and a team already comfortable operating GraphQL in production. If you're unsure, REST is the lower-risk, lower-cost starting point — and you can always add a GraphQL gateway over REST services later if the need becomes concrete.

One hybrid worth knowing: many teams keep REST for the bulk of the API and add GraphQL (or tRPC, if it's a TypeScript stack) only for the few complex, data-aggregating screens that actually need it.

If you tell me your stack, team size, and the rough number/variety of client apps, I'll give you a firmer call rather than the general one.
